statement: LangChain's core design principle is that every component — LLMs, retrievers, output parsers, chains — implements the same Runnable interface with invoke/stream/batch/ainvoke methods. This 'everything is a Runnable' philosophy means composition is always the same operation regardless of what is being composed. The WHY: this uniformity lets you swap any component without touching surrounding code, making the framework a set of interchangeable building blocks rather than a rigid pipeline.

LangChain Expression Language (LCEL) is the composition primitive that chains Runnables using the pipe operator (|). The UNSAID reason LCEL exists is not just syntactic sugar — it bakes in automatic streaming, parallel execution via RunnableParallel, and full LangSmith tracing at the composition layer itself. Any chain built with LCEL gets these behaviors for free without extra configuration; chains built with legacy LCEL-free classes do not. This is the architectural moat that made LCEL the mandatory path forward.

LangChain's package split (langchain, langchain-core, langchain-community, langchain-openai, etc.) is the most common source of ImportError and version conflict in production. The UNSAID trap: langchain-core is the stable base with no third-party dependencies; langchain is a thin meta-package; langchain-community holds integrations with looser pinning. Installing just 'langchain' pulls in langchain-core automatically, but installing only 'langchain-openai' does NOT install langchain — causing missing chain utilities. Pinning langchain and langchain-openai independently without checking their shared langchain-core constraint causes silent version mismatches.

The v0.1→v0.2→v0.3 migration path combined with Pydantic v2 is the single largest breakage source in LangChain production codebases. LangChain v0.2 dropped many legacy chain classes; v0.3 dropped Python 3.8 and fully migrated internal models to Pydantic v2. The hidden trap: custom classes subclassing BaseModel or BaseTool that worked with Pydantic v1 break silently at runtime under v0.3 because Pydantic v2 enforces stricter type coercion and changes field validators from @validator to @field_validator. Many tutorials still show v0.1 syntax, creating a false sense of compatibility when code runs but produces wrong behavior.

The 'why we removed LangChain' pattern (popularized by multiple engineering blogs in 2024) identifies over-abstraction as the core failure mode for simple use cases. LangChain's value is proportional to use-case complexity: for a single LLM call with a prompt template, the framework adds 3-4 abstraction layers with no benefit. The UNSAID signal: LangChain shines when you need retrieval, memory, multi-step tool use, or multi-model routing — its composition primitives pay off only when you have 3+ components to compose. Teams that bolt it on for single-step pipelines end up debugging framework internals instead of their actual logic.

LCEL streaming and callback propagation has a non-obvious gotcha: callbacks passed to .invoke() do NOT automatically propagate into nested Runnables created inside RunnableLambda or custom tools. The UNSAID mechanism: callbacks propagate through the chain graph only if the nested call uses the config parameter (RunnableConfig) passed into the lambda. Code that calls another chain directly inside a lambda (e.g., inner_chain.invoke(input)) instead of inner_chain.invoke(input, config) silently drops tracing and token-level streaming for all downstream steps, making LangSmith traces appear to have missing spans.

Tool and function calling schemas differ materially across providers, and LangChain's tool abstraction hides this until you hit an edge case. OpenAI accepts nested object schemas; Anthropic requires all properties be present in required when strict mode is off; Gemini rejects certain JSON Schema keywords like 'default'. The WHY: LangChain's @tool decorator generates a schema from Python type hints using Pydantic, but it does not validate or adapt that schema per-provider — it passes it through as-is. Using Optional fields, Union types, or complex nested models in tools will work on one provider and silently fail or error on another.

ConversationBufferMemory and all classes in langchain.memory are deprecated as of v0.3 and will be removed. The UNSAID problem is that they were stateful objects that mixed concerns: they stored messages AND managed the prompt injection, making them impossible to compose cleanly with LCEL. The replacement pattern is explicit message history management using RunnableWithMessageHistory or direct ChatMessageHistory objects passed through chain state. Teams migrating from ConversationBufferMemory often reproduce the same tight coupling in their replacement code because they don't understand that the deprecation was architectural, not cosmetic.

Output parsers (PydanticOutputParser, StructuredOutputParser, JsonOutputParser) fail brittlely because they rely on the LLM following format instructions in the prompt — there is no retry or schema enforcement at the protocol level. The UNSAID fix that most teams miss: use .with_structured_output() on the chat model instead of a separate output parser. This method uses the provider's native function-calling or JSON-mode API to enforce structure at the model API level, making it 10x more reliable than prompt-based parsing. Output parsers remain useful only for models that don't support native structured output.

AgentExecutor is the legacy agent runtime and is superseded by LangGraph for all non-trivial agent workflows. The UNSAID distinction: AgentExecutor is a fixed loop (reason → act → observe, repeat) with no ability to branch, retry individual steps, or maintain complex state machines. LangGraph models the agent as an explicit state graph where you define nodes and edges, enabling conditional branching, parallel tool calls, human-in-the-loop interruption, and checkpointing. The migration trigger is not complexity per se — it is whenever you need any control flow beyond linear ReAct: if yes, use LangGraph from day one rather than retrofitting AgentExecutor.
